question: Explain the 'heapify' operation. Describe its purpose, how it works on a single node, and state its time complexity.
expectedPoints: Purpose: To maintain the heap property (either max-heap or min-heap) at a given node i, assuming the subtrees rooted at its left and right children are already heaps.

Process: Compares the element at node i with its left and right children. If the heap property is violated (e.g., a child is larger than the parent in a max-heap), it swaps the element with the larger child and recursively heapifies the affected subtree.
Time Complexity: O(log n), as the operation may need to traverse the height of the tree.

question: Walk through the steps of the heapsort algorithm on the array [4, 1, 3, 2, 16, 9]. Show the state of the array after building the initial max-heap and after the first two extraction (swap and heapify) steps.
expectedPoints: Building the initial max-heap: Start from the last non-leaf node and heapify backwards. The final max-heap built from [4,1,3,2,16,9] should be [16, 4, 9, 2, 1, 3] or an equivalent valid max-heap structure.

First extraction step: Swap the root (max element, 16) with the last element (3). The array becomes [3, 4, 9, 2, 1, 16]. Then, call heapify on the new root (index 0) for the reduced heap (size 5), resulting in the heap [9, 4, 3, 2, 1] within the array [9, 4, 3, 2, 1, 16].

Second extraction step: Swap the new root (9) with the last element in the unsorted part (1). The array becomes [1, 4, 3, 2, 9, 16]. Then, call heapify on the root for the reduced heap (size 4), resulting in the heap [4, 2, 3, 1] within the array [4, 2, 3, 1, 9, 16].

question: Compare and contrast the heapsort algorithm with the mergesort and quicksort algorithms. Your answer should analyze all three in terms of time complexity (best, average, worst-case), space complexity, stability, and whether they are in-place. Discuss a key advantage and a key disadvantage of heapsort.
expectedPoints: Time Complexity: Heapsort has O(n log n) best, average, and worst-case time complexity. Mergesort also has O(n log n) for all cases. Quicksort has O(n log n) average, but a worst-case of O(n²), though this is rare with good pivot selection.

Space Complexity: Heapsort is an in-place sort, requiring only O(1) auxiliary space. Quicksort is also in-place in its common implementations, using O(log n) stack space for recursion. Mergesort requires O(n) auxiliary space for merging.
Stability: Heapsort is not stable due to the swap operations that can change the relative order of equal elements. Mergesort is stable if implemented carefully. Quicksort is generally not stable.
Key Advantage of Heapsort: Its guaranteed O(n log n) worst-case performance, making it reliable and a good choice when worst-case performance is critical, unlike quicksort.
Key Disadvantage of Heapsort: It has poor cache performance compared to quicksort and mergesort because the heapify operations access memory in non-sequential patterns, leading to more cache misses.
